feedback.text: While I agree with most of the points that are being addressed in this act, I am concerned with the lack of protection for customers in regards to the videogame industry and general internet censorship. For years it has been shown that videogame companies don't care about the playerbase of a product and only care about milking as much money as they can from it. While some of the practices are being regulated in the act, there is no mention of them terminating the online activity of any videogame without giving the customer an alternative to keep using it. The most apparent example that can be given is Ubisoft's termination of The Crew, going as far as deleting the game entirely from people's digital libraries. There has been a movement talking about this, the Stop Killing Games movement, that speaks about this with further detail, so no further detail is needed from my part. My full support is on it. As for censorship, we have already seen multiple games being taken off of their platforms. While most of them are pornographic (which isn't bad in and of itself so it's not justified) other, non-pornographic games with adult themes are being affected (mainly an indie psychological horror game called Mouthwashing). None of them were doing anything illegal, and payment processors should not have a say on what each platform is allowed to sell on their own pages. It is not their jurisdiction. Leading from that, legislation has been passed in the UK, making it mandatory to share either your personal biometrics or your personal information (mainly your ID) to access many parts of the internet. This was all supposedly in the name of "protecting the children", but it has affected harmless webpages like Wikipedia. While it's normal to want to protect the children from harmful influences, it shouldn't be the Internet's job to censor itself for them. This is a slippery slope, and the government having such control over the information their citizens are allowed to see can lead to the spread of misinformation and eventually to catastrophic consequences. The protection of the children should, logically speaking, be the parents' responsibility, and not affect the rest of the world because of it, considering the fact that having to share such important information on not-so-trustworthy pages would lead to the theft of said information by wrongdoers (or even corporations like Meta or Google), and they would eventually be sold to the highest bidder. This is not a fascist dystopia. Do not censor the Internet using the safety of our children as an excuse. Educate the parents. Thank you for reading, hope you have a beautiful day.
